title: The Soup

jokes.text: No baron has ever owned a LeBaron.
jokes.setup: Jerry riffs on how stupid car names are, starting with the Chrysler LeBaron.
jokes.punchline: The name implies aristocracy but no actual baron has ever owned one — the branding is pure pretension.
jokes.explanation: Clean, economical observational joke. The irony of a mass-market car having an aristocratic name is a sharp observation delivered with perfect compression. Strong opener for the stand-up bit, and gains ironic resonance as the episode's LeBaron plot unfolds.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What about the Probe? The Ford Probe. That's a weird name for a car. It sounds like something from a science fiction movie. 'We're going to probe you with the new Ford Probe.' I don't want to be probed. I don't want anyone probing me, let alone in a Ford.

jokes.text: The Integra. Oh, integrity? No, Integra.
jokes.setup: Car companies mangle positive words into car names.
jokes.punchline: They almost get to 'integrity' but stop short — implying they can't quite commit to the actual virtue.
jokes.explanation: The implied joke — car companies gesturing at integrity without having it — lands cleanly in very few words. The subtext elevates what could be a simple wordplay joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Limited. LTD. Limited to what? Limited to what they can sell.

jokes.text: The Supra or the Impreza. Well, I hope it's not a 'lemona.' Or you'll be hearing from my 'lawya.'
jokes.setup: Continuing the car-name mangling bit — Supra, Impreza follow the pattern.
jokes.punchline: Jerry extends the mangled-word game to 'lemon' and 'lawyer,' going full absurdist with a fake accent.
jokes.explanation: The bit escalates playfully and the double wordplay with the fake accent works, though it's a bit more gimmicky than the earlier car-name jokes. A solid closer for the opening stand-up run.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I hate about car names? They take a perfectly good word and just mangle it. Like 'Altima' — what is that? It's not a word. It sounds like they were trying to say 'optimum' but gave up halfway through.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And then there's 'Maxima' — oh, we're just adding 'a' to everything now? What's next, 'Goodima'? 'Reliabima'?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The whole thing is cynical marketing. They don't want you to think about what the word actually means. They just want something that sounds vaguely impressive and Latin-y.

jokes.text: Got a few more miles on it, but the previous owner was Jon Voight.
jokes.setup: George is firm on an '89 Volvo and rejects a LeBaron. Vic mentions it has more miles.
jokes.punchline: The celebrity owner is offered as the offset for extra miles — an absurd value proposition.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's inciting comedic premise and it lands perfectly. The casual drop of 'Jon Voight' after the setup about extra mileage is a masterclass in comic misdirection. The joke works because it's so specific — not a huge movie star, not a household name, just Jon Voight — which makes the absurdity completely believable and funny. The episode's entire comedic architecture rests on this moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking, what if you went to a lemonade stand and the guy said 'That'll be five dollars.' And you said 'Five dollars? For lemonade?' And he said 'Well, it's not lemonade, it's lemona. We couldn't afford the 'de.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Or you go to a lawyer and he says 'I'm not a lawyer, I'm a lawya. I dropped the 'er' - I was going through some financial difficulties.'

jokes.text: Jon Voight? [Long pause — George clearly turns and stares]
jokes.setup: Vic casually mentions Jon Voight was the previous owner.
jokes.punchline: George's slow, silent absorption of this information — the pause before he bites — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The beat of silence and the repeated 'Jon Voight?' before the scene cut is perfectly characteristic of George — his vanity and desire for a celebrity connection overriding all rational car-buying instincts. The character integration here is exceptional; this joke only works with George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I want the Typhoon. I've done the research. I know what I want.

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: The Typhoon. Good choice. Now, what about N.I.?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: N.I.? What's N.I.?

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: N.I. — No Interest.

jokes.text: He's got that great apartment on 77th Street and they overlook where they inflate those huge balloons for the Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade.
jokes.setup: Elaine mentions Tim Whatley's Thanksgiving party.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's enthusiasm for the party is entirely about the parade balloon view — she has a crush on Tim, but the apartment is what seals it.
jokes.explanation: Functional scene-setting joke that also plants the Macy's Parade thread. Mild humor in that the apartment's utility is for a childlike purpose (watching balloon inflation). Not a major laugh but solid.

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: This car was owned by a little old lady who only drove it on Sundays.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Really? Who?

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: Stevie Nicks.

jokes.text: He's a dentist. You don't wanna go out with a dentist. He'll always be criticising your brushing technique. Away from the gums.
jokes.setup: Elaine says she has a crush on Tim Whatley the dentist and wonders why he won't ask her out.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's warning about dentist-dating devolves into a demonstration: 'Away from the gums.' — an involuntary dentist-mode imitation.
jokes.explanation: 'Away from the gums' is a genuinely sharp joke — Jerry slides from advice-giving into dentist impression mid-sentence without announcing it. The shift is seamless and the specificity of the dental instruction makes it memorable and quotable. Strong Jerry-mode observational comedy that also foreshadows the episode's dentist plot.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't date a dentist. Think about it. A dentist is constantly looking at people's teeth. How are you gonna have a normal relationship with someone who is always thinking about the condition of your teeth?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What do you mean? That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You're eating a sandwich, you're trying to enjoy yourself, and they're just staring at your molars. 'You know, you really should floss more.' Even when you're intimate, they're thinking about your gums.

jokes.text: A LeBaron? I thought Consumer said Volvo was the car. What consumer? I'm the consumer.
jokes.setup: George shows up with a LeBaron instead of the Volvo he said he'd buy.
jokes.punchline: George dismisses 'Consumer Reports' as an abstraction — he IS the consumer, so his irrational impulse is the only data point that matters.
jokes.explanation: 'What consumer? I'm the consumer.' is a brilliantly compact piece of George Costanza logic — total self-serving rationalization delivered with conviction. It perfectly encapsulates how George operates and is highly quotable. The joke earns its high score through character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, Consumer Reports says you should never buy a used car from a private party.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, I'm a consumer. I bought a used car from a private party. So I guess I'm not supposed to exist.

jokes.text: But it was good enough for Mr. Jon Voight.
jokes.setup: Jerry says the LeBaron 'seems like a strange choice.'
jokes.punchline: George invokes Jon Voight with mock dignity as the ultimate validation — 'Mr. Jon Voight.'
jokes.explanation: The addition of 'Mr.' before Jon Voight is a perfect comedic touch — George treating a B-list celebrity with formal reverence. Pure George character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I didn't buy this car to meet celebrities.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I'm not a comedian.

jokes.text: I've never even seen him in a car. Look at his movies. Deliverance — canoe. Midnight Cowboy — boots. Runaway Train — runaway train.
jokes.setup: George defends his car purchase by claiming Jon Voight is associated with vehicular transport.
jokes.punchline: His film inventory escalates perfectly: canoe, boots, then the absurdist logical trap of 'Runaway Train... runaway train' — the title IS the punchline.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. The list structure is perfectly calibrated — 'canoe' and 'boots' build the joke's logic, then 'Runaway Train... runaway train' collapses it with self-referential absurdity. The triple structure, the escalation, and the deadpan delivery make this a textbook Seinfeld joke. The pause implicit in the repetition is comic gold. Approaches the quality threshold for a truly exceptional sitcom joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're obsessed with Jon Voight.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I am not obsessed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You've seen Runaway Train like fifteen times.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a great movie!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's the same movie every time. Jon Voight is on a train, and it's runaway. Hence, Runaway Train.

jokes.text: If they don't get business, they'll shut down and make way for one of those gourmet coffee or cookie stores.
jokes.setup: Kramer wants Jerry to support Mom and Pop's shoe repair by bringing sneakers.
jokes.punchline: The threat of replacement by a yuppie gourmet coffee/cookie store — Kramer's neighborhood anxiety is comic in its earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the Mom and Pop plotline. Mild social commentary humor, not a major laugh but plants the plot efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, we're losing all the mom-and-pop stores. Pretty soon it's gonna be nothing but chains.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's a shame.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It is a shame. These big companies, they come in, they destroy the little guy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, what do you want me to do about it?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Shop at the mom-and-pop stores.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why would I do that? The chains are cheaper and they're open later.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Because you're killing America!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm killing America? I'm just trying to buy socks.

jokes.text: Well, what's so great about a mom-and-pop store? Let me tell you, if my mom and pop ran a store, I wouldn't shop there.
jokes.setup: Kramer lectures Jerry and George about supporting mom-and-pop stores and calls them yuppies.
jokes.punchline: Jerry subverts the saccharine rhetoric about mom-and-pop stores with the dark admission that his own parents would be terrible shopkeepers.
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp deflation of sentimental rhetoric. Jerry punctures the entire concept of 'mom and pop' as inherently virtuous by applying it literally to his own family. The joke gains extra resonance because it ironically foreshadows the con-artist mom and pop. Well-constructed and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gotta go to this guy, he's the best. He cobbled my shoes.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cobbled? What are you talking about? You don't cobble shoes. You resole them, you reheel them. Cobbled is what you do to stones. You cobble stones together.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, that's what he did.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He didn't cobble your shoes, Kramer.

jokes.text: These belong to my neighbour, Jerry Seinfeld, the comedian.
jokes.setup: Kramer drops off sneakers at the cobbler shop.
jokes.punchline: Kramer casually identifies Jerry by his full name and occupation to strangers — a mix of name-dropping and the show's self-referential humor.
jokes.explanation: Mild meta-joke and character comedy — Kramer's oblivious formality in dropping Jerry's name is charming. Also sets up the later garage-sale callback.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, I think it's because you're afraid of commitment. You see, each sneaker represents a different version of yourself that you're trying on, but you can never fully commit to one identity. It's a defense mechanism.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, they're sneakers.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Exactly. You're a tortured soul, Jerry.

jokes.text: Well, he's got a Peter Pan complex.
jokes.setup: Mom is astonished by how many sneakers there are.
jokes.punchline: The cobbler explains the excessive sneaker collection as a Peter Pan complex — refusing to grow up, evidenced by adult-sized children's shoes.
jokes.explanation: Quick, economical joke with a nice psychological edge. The 'Peter Pan complex' as an explanation for too many sneakers is absurd but has internal logic. Lands as a brisk background character joke.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what? Jon Voight is a better actor than Liam Neeson.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Playing a heroic guy, an upstanding guy - that's easy. But playing a sleazy, slimy, despicable guy? That takes real talent.

jokes.text: Hey, what's with your ceiling? You got wires sticking out every which way. It looks dangerous. You should call an electrician. In the 48 years we've been here, I don't think we've ever called an electrician. Well, you should. This place could blow any minute.
jokes.setup: Kramer is at the cobbler shop and notices dangerous exposed wiring.
jokes.punchline: The shop has gone 48 years without an electrician — Kramer's warning sets up the later electrical crisis that inadvertently destroys the business.
jokes.explanation: The dark irony is that Kramer's well-intentioned intervention destroys the very business he came to save. The 'could blow any minute' line is prophetic and blackly comic. Rewatch value is high because you know what's coming.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I stepped in gum.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You stepped in gum? Where?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Right outside.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well don't come near my car! I just had it detailed!

jokes.text: Have you gotten all the salt off those pretzels yet?
jokes.setup: Elaine is at work with Mr. Pitt.
jokes.punchline: Elaine is employed as an assistant to remove salt from pretzels — an absurdly menial task for an office job, played completely straight.
jokes.explanation: Mr. Pitt is an instantly hilarious recurring character — his aristocratic eccentricity (having his assistant de-salt pretzels) played with total earnestness is great absurdist character comedy. The deadpan makes it land hard.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm gonna walk down Fifth Avenue, gonna flag down a yellow cab... No wait, I'm gonna get a nice car, a Buick maybe, something with good upholstery...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: They call me Midnight Cowboy but I should call myself Midnight Car Boy because I'm obsessed with Jon Voight's automobile situation in that film...

jokes.text: What in blazes are you listening to?
jokes.setup: Elaine is listening to a big-band radio contest at work.
jokes.punchline: 'What in blazes' — Mr. Pitt's antiquated vocabulary is itself a joke, marking him as comically out of time.
jokes.explanation: Mr. Pitt's language is consistently from another era, and 'what in blazes' gets a mild laugh from pure character voice authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Where'd you get the cowboy boots?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Oh, I did a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A thing?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I was the voice of a character on a children's show. They paid me in boots.

jokes.text: My father was a stern man. He forbade us to participate in any activities that he thought were associated with the common man. The Thanksgiving Day Parade was first on the list.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks why Mr. Pitt wants to hold a rope under Woody Woodpecker.
jokes.punchline: His desperate desire to hold a parade balloon rope is a lifelong rebellion against his repressive father — absurd stakes for such a mundane goal.
jokes.explanation: The comedic gap between the grandeur of the backstory (stern father, forbidden pleasures) and the triviality of the goal (holding a rope under Woody Woodpecker) is exquisite. The joke works on multiple levels: it's character-specific, absurdist, and darkly funny about repressive parenting. Strong Seinfeld-style absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hey, I'm not that bad. I'm like Jon Voight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're nothing like Jon Voight.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, he's not a likable guy either!

jokes.text: Will you shut up? I can't hear!
jokes.setup: Mr. Pitt keeps asking Elaine if she knows the next song on the radio contest.
jokes.punchline: Elaine snaps at her boss — a completely inappropriate workplace outburst driven by her sudden obsession with the contest.
jokes.explanation: The cringe of Elaine barking at her employer mid-obsession is a nice character beat. The setup has built the tension well.

dialogue.speaker: Pop
dialogue.line: This is all Kramer's fault!

dialogue.speaker: Mom
dialogue.line: How is this Kramer's fault? We've owned this shop for 48 years.

dialogue.speaker: Pop
dialogue.line: He should have told us about the electrical code!

dialogue.speaker: Mom
dialogue.line: Pop, we knew about it.

dialogue.speaker: Pop
dialogue.line: Well, he should have made us do something about it!

jokes.text: If he had said Liam Neeson, you'd know he's making it up. Liam Neeson? How are you comparing Liam Neeson with Jon Voight? We're talking about Joe Buck. If you can play Joe Buck, Oskar Schindler's a cakewalk.
jokes.setup: Jerry doubts that the car really belonged to Jon Voight. George argues the choice of Jon Voight is too specific to be a lie.
jokes.punchline: George's film-criticism defense of Jon Voight's authenticity — culminating in 'if you can play Joe Buck, Oskar Schindler's a cakewalk' — is gloriously unhinged fanboy logic delivered with complete conviction.
jokes.explanation: This exchange is a masterpiece of George Costanza character comedy. His passionate, detailed defense of Jon Voight's acting range — used to justify a completely irrational car purchase — is precisely the kind of elaborate absurdist reasoning George deploys. 'If you can play Joe Buck, Oskar Schindler's a cakewalk' is a genuinely brilliant line: specific, unexpected, logically ridiculous, and utterly George. High quotability, very strong candidate for best moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What is this? A pencil?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I found it in the glove compartment. Look at the bite marks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Bite marks? George, it's a pencil.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Those are teeth marks. Very distinctive. I'm telling you, a celebrity bit this pencil. I can tell by the dental work.

jokes.text: Oh, look at this. I stepped in gum. You're not getting in my car with gummy shoes.
jokes.setup: George has just finished his passionate monologue about Jon Voight's car.
jokes.punchline: George immediately bars Jerry from entering 'his' (formerly Jon Voight's) car because of gum — territorial pride over an inherited mess.
jokes.explanation: George's instant possessiveness over 'Jon Voight's car' is a perfect character beat — he went from buying a stranger's car to owning it as a totem in minutes. The petty 'gummy shoes' rule is a great George moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What if I just started spelling my name wrong? Like, 'It's Jerry... with a G. G-E-R-R-Y. No wait, that's how you spell it. Okay, G-I-R-R-Y. No, that's not right either. How about J-E-R-R-E? No... J-A-R-R-Y. Yeah, that's it. From now on, I'm J-A-R-R-Y.'

jokes.text: Liam Neeson. You know he's not American.
jokes.setup: Jerry has walked away to change his shoes after being banned from the car.
jokes.punchline: George mutters a final, irrelevant Liam Neeson critique — still arguing the point to no one.
jokes.explanation: George continuing the argument alone — dismissing Liam Neeson on the grounds of citizenship — is a perfect character moment. The non sequitur addition of 'You know he's not American' as if that clinches the argument is brilliantly absurd. One of the funniest background beats in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What, you want me to believe it's the car from Kramer's dream? Next you'll tell me it's Steve Buscemi's personal vehicle!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, and I suppose that's Al Pacino's limo parked outside? With Sean Penn doing valet?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Maybe it's Cher's convertible with Madonna as the co-pilot!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Or better yet—it's actually a spaceship piloted by extraterrestrial versions of the entire cast of 'thirtysomething'!

jokes.text: Just driving round in Jon Voight's car [sung to 'Everybody's Talkin'']
jokes.setup: Jerry goes inside to change shoes; Kramer is apparently in the car.
jokes.punchline: Kramer has spontaneously commandeered the car and is singing a parody lyric to the Midnight Cowboy theme — the film George just cited — before Jerry yells at him.
jokes.explanation: This is a perfect Kramer moment — spontaneous, physical, musical, totally in his own world. The callback to 'Midnight Cowboy' (referenced in George's filmography rant) by using its iconic theme song is a brilliant layered joke. Kramer rewrites the lyric to 'just driving round in Jon Voight's car' which encapsulates the whole absurd episode premise. High rewatch and quotability value.

dialogue.speaker: Street Thug
dialogue.line: Hey, what's with the boots, cowboy? You think you're some kind of urban pioneer? You look like you're about to line dance in front of a bodega.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They're just boots.

dialogue.speaker: Street Thug
dialogue.line: Just boots? You're dressed like a Village People backup dancer who got lost on the way to the subway. Give me your wallet.

jokes.text: Where's all my sneakers? — You said take them. — Not all of them. — Well, obviously, there was a miscommunication.
jokes.setup: Jerry comes back to find ALL his sneakers are gone.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's breezy 'obviously, there was a miscommunication' for a disaster of his own making is perfect Kramer self-insulation from blame.
jokes.explanation: Kramer's total failure to register that he did something wrong — delivered as diplomatic corporate-speak ('miscommunication') — is very characteristic and funny. The casualness in the face of Jerry's implied outrage is key.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I think Jon Voight changed the spelling of his name. It used to be Jon Vo-ight, but he changed it to Jon Voight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, that's a good change. Voight is a lot zippier than Vo-ight.

jokes.text: I worked a club in Dallas once, and they couldn't pay me, so they gave me these.
jokes.setup: Kramer offers Jerry a pair of cowboy boots since all his sneakers are gone.
jokes.punchline: The boots were a payment-in-kind from a Dallas club that couldn't pay cash — the implication being Jerry's comedy gigs were not always high-paying.
jokes.explanation: Light backstory joke. The comedy club paying in cowboy boots is mildly absurd and self-deprecating. Works as a mid-level chuckle.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm trying to figure out how to word the invitation. Do I say 'Please join us' or 'You're invited' or 'We're having a party'?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Why don't you just say 'Come to my party'?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, that's too casual. It's got to have the right tone.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, nobody cares about the wording.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They don't? So what are you saying - I should use 'Please join us'?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm saying don't have a party.

jokes.text: You look like a cowboy. — I don't wanna be a cowboy.
jokes.setup: Jerry has reluctantly put on the cowboy boots.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's flat 'I don't wanna be a cowboy' is a perfectly Seinfeld-ian petulant declaration.
jokes.explanation: Short but effective. The childlike directness of 'I don't wanna be a cowboy' lands as a quick laugh. Economy is its best quality.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. You have no children?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: That's right.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But it's a 'mom and pop' store.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you can't be a mom and pop without kids. That's false advertising!

jokes.text: You didn't get one? But he called me up and asked for yours and Elaine's addresses. I'm sure that means I'm invited. Not necessarily. Why would you call someone for addresses if you're not invited to the party? That's the genius of it.
jokes.setup: George reveals he didn't get an invitation to Tim Whatley's party, even though Tim called him to get Elaine and Jerry's addresses.
jokes.punchline: 'That's the genius of it' — Jerry mirrors George's own earlier phrase (used to praise Jon Voight) back at him, now as mockery of the socially sophisticated non-invitation.
jokes.explanation: Jerry using George's own 'that's the genius of it' back at him is a tight callback that enriches both jokes. The social cruelty of using someone to get addresses to a party they're not invited to is sharp and relatable. The callback adds an extra layer that rewards attentive viewers.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. The shop owners all got together, they formed a coalition, they made a pact. They said, 'You know what? Let's all raise our prices on the same day, so nobody knows about it.' That's a conspiracy.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Exactly!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And how much did prices go up?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A nickel. A dime.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A nickel? You're telling me these shop owners got together, coordinated a massive price-fixing scheme, risked federal prosecution, all so they could raise the price of a cup of coffee a nickel?

jokes.text: Four thousand dollars! We can't afford that. Well, you have to do something about it, because it's in violation of the building code. Supposing we can't pay for it? Then I have to report you. Otherwise, I lose my license. Sorry.
jokes.setup: The electrician Kramer urged them to call has found $4,000 in code violations.
jokes.punchline: The electrician apologizes for being forced to destroy the business his report will shut down — comically insufficient 'sorry' for the damage.
jokes.explanation: Dark irony payoff from Kramer's earlier electrician advice — the 'sorry' at the end is a nicely absurd understatement for shutting down a 48-year-old business. The joke earns its impact through setup across the episode.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I was thinking about it. Jon Voight is a terrific actor. One of the best.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Jon Voight? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Come on, he's great. 'Midnight Cowboy,' 'Coming Home'...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, Jon Voight is not a good actor.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Are you out of your mind? The man is a craftsman!

jokes.text: Forty-eight years, Mom. And now we have to close. All because of that idiot and his bloody nose.
jokes.setup: Pop mourns that their 48-year business is being shut down due to electrical code violations.
jokes.punchline: The chain of causation — traced back to Kramer's nosebleed — is absurd and darkly funny. 'That idiot and his bloody nose' is a perfect encapsulation of Seinfeld's chaos-theory plot structure.
jokes.explanation: The butterfly-effect joke lands beautifully here — 48 years of business destroyed by a nosebleed visit and a well-intentioned tip. 'That idiot and his bloody nose' is a great line and a perfect Seinfeld plot-consequence moment.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What if he just shows up? What if he decides to come to my apartment?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, that's what happens to me every day.

jokes.text: Afternoon, Mom. Afternoon, Pop. You know you got a crack in the sidewalk? You ought to get that fixed.
jokes.setup: Kramer cheerfully greets Mom and Pop just as they've learned their business must close due to his earlier advice.
jokes.punchline: Completely unaware of the devastation he's caused, Kramer immediately identifies another 'problem' to fix. The structural irony is enormous.
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly constructed irony beat. Kramer arrives, all bonhomie, having inadvertently destroyed the business — and instantly spots another problem to 'helpfully' flag. The cluelessness is perfectly Kramer, the structural irony is textbook Seinfeld. One of the episode's strongest moments.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Vampire! It's a vampire!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The bite marks! Two puncture wounds! It's a vampire, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, it was Jon Voight.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Exactly! Jon Voight!

jokes.text: Come on, put the top up. — It's November. — I feel alive, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George are in the convertible LeBaron.
jokes.punchline: George insists on top-down driving in November because Jon Voight's car ownership has made him 'feel alive' — complete dissolution of rational behavior in the celebrity glow.
jokes.explanation: 'I feel alive, Jerry' is a wonderfully absurd line — George has achieved a sort of transcendence through a used car. Very George, very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm thinking, how am I gonna prove this? I go to the police, I say 'Officer, I have a bite mark.' He says 'Let me see it.' I show him my arm. He says 'That could be anybody.' So I say 'No, no, no, I have the dental records of everyone at the party!'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm walking around with a clipboard, 'Okay, who wants to go first? Come on, open wide. We're doing a little forensic work here.'

jokes.text: Pencil. Hey, you don't think? Sure. That's Jon Voight's pencil. With Jon Voight's teeth marks.
jokes.setup: George and Jerry find a pencil in the glove box of the LeBaron.
jokes.punchline: George immediately assigns the pencil celebrity provenance — 'Jon Voight's pencil. With Jon Voight's teeth marks.' — treating mundane detritus as sacred relics.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from pencil → Jon Voight's pencil → Jon Voight's teeth marks is perfect absurdist escalation. George treating car-detritus as holy relics because of potential celebrity ownership is very funny and pays off hugely later with the bite-mark comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So what do you think about Woody Woodpecker?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Pitt
dialogue.line: Well, I'll tell you something about Woody. He's a very disturbed individual. All that laughing, the manic energy - it's a cry for help. The man is clearly struggling with some very deep psychological issues.

jokes.text: Except Jon is spelled with an H. J-O-H-N. Doesn't Jon Voight spell his name J-O-N?
jokes.setup: George is convinced the car was Jon Voight's after finding the owner's manual.
jokes.punchline: Jerry notices the name is spelled 'John' not 'Jon' — the whole celebrity provenance may be based on a misidentified signature.
jokes.explanation: This is an expertly constructed reversal. The entire episode's premise pivots on a single letter — J-O-N vs J-O-H-N. The structural elegance of deflating George's entire worldview with one consonant is brilliant Seinfeld plotting. The joke is both a plot twist and a perfect punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Tim
dialogue.line: Hey Jerry, how's it going?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's going great! You know, I've been thinking about how people say 'how's it going' — nobody actually wants to know how it's going. If you really told them, they'd walk away. 'It's going terrible, Tim. My life is falling apart.' But you say 'great' and suddenly you're a well-adjusted person.

jokes.text: I'm sure Jon probably misspelled his own name. I know sometimes I spell Jerry with a G. And an I.
jokes.setup: George tries to explain away the misspelled 'John' — surely Jon Voight just misspelled his own name.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's 'I know sometimes I spell Jerry with a G. And an I.' — a devastatingly dry way to call George out on his denial.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest Jerry lines in the episode. 'I know sometimes I spell Jerry with a G. And an I.' — the pause before 'And an I' is crucial — is perfectly deadpan and devastating. It punctures George's denial with surgical precision and tremendous economy. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know Jon Voight? THE Jon Voight? The actor?

dialogue.speaker: Tim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know Jon Voight.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jon Voight the Academy Award winner? Midnight Cowboy, Coming Home?

dialogue.speaker: Tim
dialogue.line: Well, no. It's a different Jon Voight. He's an accountant in Paramus.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: An accountant? There's two Jon Voights?

jokes.text: Get out of the car! Oh, look. There's Gregory Peck's bicycle. — Get out! — And Barbara Mandrell's skateboard. — Get out!
jokes.setup: George kicks Jerry out of the car for undermining his Jon Voight fantasy.
jokes.punchline: Jerry escalates his mockery by attributing increasingly random celebrity ownership to street objects — Gregory Peck's bicycle, Barbara Mandrell's skateboard.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation and callback. Jerry's mockery of the Jon Voight premise by attributing other objects to random B-list celebrities is very funny — 'Barbara Mandrell's skateboard' is particularly inspired. The parallel structure of George's 'Get out!' responses adds rhythm. One of the episode's most memorable exchanges.

dialogue.speaker: Dentist Tim
dialogue.line: Open wide, let me see that tooth.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Mmph...

dialogue.speaker: Dentist Tim
dialogue.line: You've got some plaque buildup here. I'm going to need to use the air tool.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, what? No air tools!

dialogue.speaker: Dentist Tim
dialogue.line: It'll just take a second.

jokes.text: Hey, cowboy. Where's your horse? — Yeah, you better run.
jokes.setup: Jerry is walking the street in cowboy boots after being ejected from the car.
jokes.punchline: Teens mock Jerry for looking like a cowboy; Jerry tries to save face with 'Yeah, you better run' after they presumably flee.
jokes.explanation: Modest physical comedy payoff for the cowboy boots subplot. 'Yeah, you better run' is a mild laugh — Jerry's impotent bravado after humiliation. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: Hi Jerry, it's your mother. I'm calling from the garage sale.

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: You know those sneakers you left here? Well, your father and I decided to sell them.

dialogue.speaker: Phone caller
dialogue.line: We've already made twelve dollars!

jokes.text: Well, anyway, again, I'm sorry about throwing you out of the car. — You really seemed to enjoy it. — It was kind of fun.
jokes.setup: George apologizes (perfunctorily) for ejecting Jerry.
jokes.punchline: George admits it was 'kind of fun' — completely honest and un-self-aware.
jokes.explanation: George's refreshingly honest admission is a nice character beat. The non-apology apology is very George. Quick, effective.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I don't understand about parades? Why do they always have marching bands? I mean, why do we need a marching band? We already have music. We have recorded music. We have CDs. We have the radio. Why do we need guys in funny hats walking down the street playing tubas? And why are they marching? Where are they going? They're not going anywhere. They're just walking back and forth. It's like, 'We're going to play this terrible music, AND we're going to make it difficult.'

jokes.text: You know, J-O-N is a lot zippier.
jokes.setup: George theorizes that Jon Voight changed the spelling of his name for show business.
jokes.punchline: 'J-O-N is a lot zippier' — George now defending Jon Voight's brand decisions as if they're personal friends.
jokes.explanation: George's pivot from doubt to advocating for Jon Voight's branding decisions is perfectly in character. 'A lot zippier' is an absurd bit of celebrity-brand analysis from someone in complete denial.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You ever notice how people wave differently depending on the situation? Like, at a parade, it's this big, enthusiastic wave. 'Hello! Hello!' At a funeral, it's just a slight nod. Very subtle. Then there's the car wave when someone lets you in traffic. That's a quick little finger thing. At the airport, when you're saying goodbye, that's the long, drawn-out wave where your arm gets tired. And then there's the amusement park train wave—that's special. People just lose their minds on those trains. 'Woooo! Hi, Mom!' Like they're being deported.

jokes.text: I've got the entire Yankee organisation at my disposal. — He'll dispose of it.
jokes.setup: George says he can use Yankees resources to verify if the car really belonged to Jon Voight.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's flat rejoinder: 'He'll dispose of it' — a perfectly timed dig at George's misuse of his Yankees job.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's wordplay on 'at my disposal / he'll dispose of it' is a quick, sharp joke that also undercuts George's self-important use of Yankees resources for petty personal projects. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're going to the party?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got my invitation last week.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You didn't get one?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, I... I'm sure it's coming.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not coming, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How do you know?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because everyone got one. Everyone but you.

jokes.text: Did he emphasise 'Jerry' or 'bring'? Did he say, 'Why would Jerry bring anything?' Or, 'Why would Jerry bring anything?' — I think he emphasised 'would.'
jokes.setup: Jerry asks Lois (his secretary at Yankees) to subtly probe whether he's invited to Tim Whatley's party.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's hyper-specific analysis of which word was emphasized collapses when Lois reveals the emphasized word was the neutral 'would' — deflating all of Jerry's interpretation attempts.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautiful comedy-of-obsession bit. Jerry's escalating analysis of word emphasis — essentially trying to decode an inscrutable social signal — is very relatable and well-escalated. The punchline ('I think he emphasised would') perfectly deflates all the neurotic effort. The joke structure is tight and the payoff is satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Tim
dialogue.line: So, would you like to go out with me sometime?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What? I can't hear you!

dialogue.speaker: Tim
dialogue.line: I said, would you like to go out with me?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, the mozzarella sticks are great!

dialogue.speaker: Tim
dialogue.line: No, I'm asking you on a date!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A gate? There's no gate here!

jokes.text: You know what? The hell with his party. I don't wanna go to begin with.
jokes.setup: Jerry learns his invitation status remains ambiguous.
jokes.punchline: Instant defensive pivot — of course he wants to go, but face-saving requires pretending he doesn't.
jokes.explanation: Standard sitcom defensive bluster, but in character. Mild laugh, mostly plot-propulsive.

jokes.text: Mom and Pop aren't even a mom and pop? It was all an act, Jerry. They conned us, and they scored bigtime.
jokes.setup: Kramer reports that Mom and Pop vanished along with all of Jerry's sneakers.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that they weren't actually a mom-and-pop couple — it was a long con — is absurdist and darkly funny.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the beloved neighborhood cobbler is actually a long-con operation is perfectly absurd and pays off both the emotional investment (48 years!) and Jerry's earlier cynical joke about mom-and-pop stores. 'They scored bigtime' — delivered about stolen sneakers — is a great line.

jokes.text: So Mom and Pop's plan was to move into the neighbourhood... establish trust... for 48 years... and then run off with Jerry's sneakers?
jokes.setup: Kramer has just explained the apparent long con by Mom and Pop.
jokes.punchline: Jerry slowly unpacks the absurd timeline — 48 years of planning to steal some sneakers. The escalating pause structure is the joke.
jokes.explanation: This is arguably the funniest single moment in the episode. The deliberate, pause-loaded unpacking of the absurd con — 48 years of trust-building to steal Jerry Seinfeld's sneakers — is a perfect Seinfeld absurdist escalation. The structure (establish trust... for 48 years... and then run off with Jerry's sneakers?) achieves maximum comedy through understatement, timing, and the monstrous disproportion between the effort and the reward. This is the kind of joke that makes the show legendary.

jokes.text: Jon Voight Day. — Jon Voight? The actor? — I make a motion that we have no more of these meetings that have been initiated by George Costanza.
jokes.setup: George pitches special promotional days at Yankee Stadium and offers Jon Voight Day.
jokes.punchline: The executive's response — a formal motion to ban George-initiated meetings — is a hilariously bureaucratic overreaction to what is clearly a pattern of George's bad ideas.
jokes.explanation: George's Jon Voight obsession has now infected his professional life. The executive's formal parliamentary motion against George's meetings is beautifully over-the-top and implies a long history of George's terrible ideas. Very strong character comedy.

jokes.text: I suppose if I had suggested Liam Neeson Day, you'd all be patting me on the back.
jokes.setup: George is shot down on Jon Voight Day.
jokes.punchline: George's Liam Neeson fixation returns — he's still convinced Liam Neeson would be a worse choice, and is now bitterly resentful about it.
jokes.explanation: The Liam Neeson running gag pays off perfectly here. George's wounded dignity at his Jon Voight preference being dismissed — while still maintaining the Liam Neeson beef — is a great sustained character joke. The callback rewards viewers who've been tracking the bit.

jokes.text: Jon Voight! Hey, Jon Voight! Hey, listen. Can I ask you something?
jokes.setup: Kramer spots a man he believes is Jon Voight getting into a cab.
jokes.punchline: Kramer runs after a cab screaming 'Jon Voight!' in the street — committed physical comedy that results in him getting bitten.
jokes.explanation: Kramer pursuing a man through the street yelling 'Jon Voight!' is great physical comedy. The result — getting bitten — is a perfect absurdist consequence and sets up the bite-mark subplot beautifully.

jokes.text: Jon Voight! Hey, listen. Can I ask you something? [Jon Voight's cab drives away / Kramer is left standing there]
jokes.setup: Kramer spots what appears to be the real Jon Voight on the street.
jokes.punchline: Kramer frantically chases the cab trying to ask about the car, fails, and is left standing in the street — the physical futility is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy beat that sets up the critical Kramer-gets-bitten moment. Functional as plot propulsion and has slapstick energy.

jokes.text: Jerry, for all I know, this guy went out of his way to not invite you. How am I gonna feel if I show up with an uninvited, unwelcome intruder? — The way I feel when I go places with you?
jokes.setup: George hedges about going to the party with Jerry in case Jerry is genuinely uninvited.
jokes.punchline: Jerry turns George's concern back on him — George is the perpetual 'uninvited, unwelcome intruder' everywhere.
jokes.explanation: Jerry's comeback here is perfectly timed and deeply character-specific — it implies a long history of George being exactly that. The line lands hard because it's both a sick burn and completely plausible given everything we know about George.

jokes.text: He was a little standoffish.
jokes.setup: George excitedly asks if Kramer talked to Jon Voight after seeing him.
jokes.punchline: Kramer euphemistically describes the man biting him as being 'a little standoffish' — spectacular understatement.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'He was a little standoffish' as a description of being bitten by a man in a cab is a perfect Kramer understatement. The economy is flawless — maximum information delivered with minimum words, maximum comedy. This line would be quoted forever in any other context.

jokes.text: I couldn't. His cab pulled away. But he did make an impression on me. / Look. / What? / His tooth marks. He bit me.
jokes.setup: Kramer says Jon Voight 'made an impression on him.'
jokes.punchline: He literally shows the bite mark — 'he bit me' — as the impression. The idiom is literalized perfectly.
jokes.explanation: The literalization of 'made an impression' is a textbook Seinfeld misdirection joke. The setup feels like a regular idiom, the punchline re-frames it as literal physical evidence. Beautifully constructed.

jokes.text: Look. — What? — His tooth marks. He bit me. — Jon Voight bit you? — What is he, a vampire?
jokes.setup: Kramer reveals his arm was bitten by the man he chased.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from tooth marks → vampire accusation is rapid and funny — Jerry's 'What is he, a vampire?' is an instinctive and absurd response.
jokes.explanation: Quick-fire absurdist exchange. 'What is he, a vampire?' is a funny instinctive reaction to the bite revelation. Solid comedic escalation.

jokes.text: You're showing up at that party with a chewed-up pencil and Kramer's gnarled arm?
jokes.setup: George wants to use the tooth marks on Kramer's arm and Jon Voight's pencil to verify car ownership.
jokes.punchline: Jerry summarizes the evidence plan — 'a chewed-up pencil and Kramer's gnarled arm' — as a party entrance strategy.
jokes.explanation: 'Kramer's gnarled arm' is a wonderful phrase. The image of showing up to a Thanksgiving party with a bitten pencil and a bitten arm as 'evidence' of a car's celebrity ownership is perfectly absurd. The absurdist plotting of Seinfeld at its best.

jokes.text: These are the balloons? Big deal. All I see is Woody Woodpecker. — You got a problem with Woody Woodpecker? — Yeah. What is he, some sort of an instigator? That's right. He's a troublemaker.
jokes.setup: Mr. Pitt arrives at the parade balloon inflation, having worked so hard to get here.
jokes.punchline: Having achieved his life's dream of attending the balloon inflation, Mr. Pitt immediately gripes about Woody Woodpecker being a 'troublemaker.'
jokes.explanation: Mr. Pitt dismissing the fulfillment of his childhood dream by immediately finding a grievance — Woody Woodpecker as 'troublemaker' — is exquisite character comedy. The Proustian buildup (stern father, forbidden pleasures) collapsing into aristocratic complaint is brilliant. One of the episode's best character moments.

jokes.text: Hey, did you get my message? — What? I can't hear a word you're saying. My head is still ringing.
jokes.setup: Elaine and George try to communicate at the loud parade balloon inflation.
jokes.punchline: The running gag of characters unable to communicate over the parade noise — Elaine's head is ringing from the Dixieland Deli (implied noise trauma).
jokes.explanation: Functional comedic device enabling the party scene misunderstandings. Not a strong joke on its own but serves the plot structure well.

jokes.text: Elaine, would you marry me? — I told you, I can't hear a word. — All right. Forget it.
jokes.setup: Elaine keeps saying she can't hear amid the parade noise.
jokes.punchline: A random person proposes marriage to Elaine; she literally can't hear it; he drops the whole thing. An entire relationship arc in 10 seconds.
jokes.explanation: A lovely quick absurdist joke — a marriage proposal completely lost to ambient noise, discarded with 'All right. Forget it.' The economy is near-perfect for what it achieves. A funny, throwaway moment that rewards quick-witted viewers.

jokes.text: Hey, is that Jerry Seinfeld? — He didn't come with us.
jokes.setup: Jerry has shown up to a party he wasn't invited to.
jokes.punchline: George and Kramer instantly disavow any connection to Jerry to preserve their own social standing.
jokes.explanation: George and Kramer's instant betrayal of Jerry is character-perfect and quick. 'He didn't come with us' delivered with complete social self-preservation instinct is very funny.

jokes.text: Jerry. I didn't think you'd show. — Did you say, 'Jerry, I didn't think you'd show,' or 'Jerry, I didn't think you'd show.'
jokes.setup: Tim greets Jerry at the party.
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately applies his word-emphasis parsing obsession to Tim's greeting — the same game he played with Lois's report — now live and in the moment.
jokes.explanation: Strong callback — Jerry is now doing his emphasis-parsing in real time, directly to the person he's been obsessing over. The joke builds on the earlier Lois scene and escalates the neurosis nicely.

jokes.text: I'm really glad you came. — What? — I'm really glad you came.
jokes.setup: The parade noise has made Elaine partially deaf.
jokes.punchline: Tim has to repeat his sincere compliment twice because Elaine can't hear — deflating the romantic moment.
jokes.explanation: Running gag continuation — mild cringe as the romantic moment is killed by Elaine's hearing problem. Functional but not a strong joke on its own.

jokes.text: Elaine, I've been wanting to ask you... would you like to go out with me New Year's Eve? — Thanks.
jokes.setup: Tim finally gets to ask Elaine out.
jokes.punchline: Elaine can't hear and responds with 'Thanks' — completely missing the date invitation that Jerry warned her she'd never get.
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural comedy — Elaine has been hoping Tim would ask her out all episode, and when he finally does, she mishears it and says 'Thanks.' Jerry's dentist warning is now irrelevant since she'll never know he asked. The irony is layered and satisfying.

jokes.text: Get the pencil out of your mouth! You're destroying Jon Voight's teeth marks.
jokes.setup: George presents the evidence plan (pencil + Kramer's arm) to the dentist at the party.
jokes.punchline: George is more concerned with preserving Jon Voight's teeth marks on a pencil than with Kramer's actual injury.
jokes.explanation: George's priorities — celebrity pencil preservation over human wellbeing — is a perfect character beat. The urgency of 'You're destroying Jon Voight's teeth marks!' is a great line that encapsulates his entire arc.

jokes.text: Jon Voight, the actor? — No. The periodontist.
jokes.setup: George excitedly asks if the dentist knows Jon Voight the actor after the dentist confirms he went to dental school with Jon Voight.
jokes.punchline: There is another Jon Voight — a periodontist — entirely. The celebrity provenance of the car collapses completely.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structured joke and a perfect Seinfeld payoff. The entire elaborate premise — George's obsession, the Liam Neeson debate, Jon Voight Day, the bite-mark comparison — is demolished in four words: 'No. The periodontist.' The misdirection was seeded from the very first scene, and the callback to the J-O-H-N spelling makes it retroactively inevitable. Economy, structure, and payoff are all near-perfect. This is a 9+ joke.

jokes.text: You popped Woody Woodpecker! — Hey, who invited you anyway? You're a troublemaker.
jokes.setup: Mr. Pitt is holding the rope for Woody Woodpecker balloon — his life's dream.
jokes.punchline: Mr. Pitt apparently causes the balloon to deflate. He then turns his own label ('troublemaker') onto the person accusing him — completing the full Woody Woodpecker character arc.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural payoff. Mr. Pitt achieves his dream, immediately destroys it (popping Woody Woodpecker), and then recycles his own 'troublemaker' epithet onto his accuser. The irony is layered: the man who called Woody a troublemaker becomes the troublemaker at the Woody Woodpecker balloon. Callbacks the earlier 'instigator' line with perfect precision. One of the best payoffs in the episode.

jokes.text: Looks like Woody Woodpecker is running out of air. In fact, he's collapsing. Those kids look pretty disappointed. Especially that big kid up in the front. How old is he?
jokes.setup: The Woody Woodpecker balloon is deflating at the parade.
jokes.punchline: The announcer's increasingly concerned observations — particularly 'especially that big kid up in the front, how old is he?' — suggest an adult Seinfeld character is crying over a balloon.
jokes.explanation: The announcer's confusion about the 'big kid' is a subtle joke implying Mr. Pitt or another adult is visibly distraught. The deadpan news-report format applied to balloon deflation is very Seinfeld.

jokes.text: Where are they? — Parsippany, New Jersey. — Let's go. — My car's in the shop. — How are we getting to Parsippany?
jokes.setup: Jerry finds out where Mom and Pop are selling his sneakers.
jokes.punchline: The grand revenge mission immediately hits a wall: Kramer's car is in the shop — because he was using Jon Voight's LeBaron, which presumably created the problem.
jokes.explanation: The Parsippany punchline (New Jersey as comic destination) and the immediate logistical failure are mild laughs. The 'how are we getting to Parsippany?' is the real joke — the quest ends before it begins.

jokes.text: Jerry... these nosebleeds are starting again. Maybe we should get you to a hospital. — I ain't going to no Bellevue.
jokes.setup: They try to mobilize to Parsippany; Pop suddenly has a nosebleed.
jokes.punchline: Pop's nosebleed — the very thing that started this whole chain of events — recurs at the end, and his refusal to go to Bellevue (psychiatric hospital) adds a dark comic layer.
jokes.explanation: The nosebleed callback is a perfect Seinfeld structural loop — the thing that destroyed the business bookends the episode's resolution attempt. 'I ain't going to no Bellevue' implies the nosebleeds are a known problem with psychiatric-adjacent implications. Nice dark comedy undercurrent.

jokes.text: The marching band is a perfect example of taking something bad and making it difficult too.
jokes.setup: Jerry does closing stand-up at the parade.
jokes.punchline: 'Taking something bad and making it difficult too' — the marching band is bad AND requires unnecessary effort. The inverted logic is sharp.
jokes.explanation: One of the stronger stand-up bits. 'Taking something bad and making it difficult too' is a genuinely clever formulation — the marching band is bad music AND physically demanding, for no apparent gain. The logic is tight and the line is very quotable.

jokes.text: Why does the band have to march? We're not moving. Maybe if they hold still, we could all leave. Is that why they do it? People try and get away. 'No, you don't. I'm right with you. Right with you, buddy.'
jokes.setup: Extension of the marching band bit — why does the band march?
jokes.punchline: The paranoid theory: the band marches to prevent audience escape, voiced as an aggressively cheerful band member. Jerry doing the band member's voice is the escalation.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the marching band premise. The implied conspiracy — the band follows you — and Jerry's cheerful band-member voice work well. Strong stand-up writing, though slightly extended.

jokes.text: Why does the band have to march? We're not moving. / Maybe if they hold still, we could all leave. / Is that why they do it? People try and get away. / 'No, you don't. I'm right with you. Right with you, buddy.'
jokes.setup: Jerry is riffing on why marching bands have to march.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'we're not moving' to the theory that bands march specifically to prevent people from leaving — with the imagined confrontational band member — is absurdist and funny.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from observation to absurdist theory — the band is pursuing fleeing audience members — is characteristic Jerry riffing. 'Right with you, buddy' is a funny characterization of an imaginary confrontational tuba player.

jokes.text: The human urge to wave at total strangers just moving by is very strong, isn't it? Parades and ocean liners and those little trains that go through amusement parks. It's always that bittersweet, kind of hello-goodbye combination wave. I've never seen these people before in my life, and they're leaving.
jokes.setup: Jerry observes the universality of waving at passing strangers.
jokes.punchline: The bittersweet 'hello-goodbye wave' — the melancholy of waving at people you'll never see again, immediately after meeting them. 'I've never seen these people before in my life, and they're leaving.'
jokes.explanation: This is Jerry's best stand-up moment in the episode — genuinely insightful, slightly melancholy, and perfectly observational. The 'hello-goodbye wave' formulation is novel and the kicker line ('I've never seen these people before in my life, and they're leaving') is quietly devastating. Strong closing stand-up material.

tmdb_overview: Jerry pays dearly for a "free" suit from a comic; Elaine has a beau who's no gentleman; George's way with words ends his relationship with a waitress.
insight: Soup Nazi's introduction launches character comedy gold despite subpar 74 impact score.
